name: Wheat
description: A cool-season cereal grass widely grown for its grain. One of the world's most important staple crops, used for bread, pasta and animal feed.

tips.body: Sow in autumn for winter wheat or early spring for spring wheat, at 2–4 cm depth.
Prefers well-drained loam or clay-loam soils with good organic matter content.
Critical water demand periods are tillering, heading and grain-fill. Avoid waterlogging.
Harvest when grain moisture drops to 14% and straw turns golden yellow.
Monitor for rust diseases, aphids and Hessian fly. Rotate varieties to resist disease.
Applying nitrogen fertiliser at tillering significantly improves yield and protein content.

name: Rice
description: A semi-aquatic cereal crop and the primary staple food for over half the world's population. Thrives in warm, humid conditions with flooded paddy fields.

tips.body: Transplant seedlings at 25–35 days old into flooded paddies, or direct-seed in standing water.
Clay and clay-loam soils are ideal as they retain water. Avoid sandy or well-drained soils.
Maintain 5–10 cm of standing water during vegetative and reproductive stages.
Harvest 30 days after flowering when 80–85% of grains have turned golden.
Watch for stem borers, blast disease and brown plant hopper. Use resistant varieties.
Intermittent flooding (AWD technique) can reduce water use by 30% with minimal yield loss.

name: Barley
description: A hardy cereal grain used for animal feed, malt production and human food. One of the most drought-tolerant cereal crops.

tips.body: Sow early spring or autumn at 3–5 cm depth. Spring barley matures faster than winter types.
Tolerates slightly alkaline soils well. Avoid waterlogged conditions.
Needs moisture at germination and heading but tolerates dry conditions better than wheat.
Harvest at grain moisture of 18–20%; dry to 14% for safe storage.
Powdery mildew and net blotch are common. Treat seed before sowing.
High soil pH (up to 8.0) is better tolerated by barley than most other cereal crops.

name: Maize (corn)
description: A warm-season cereal and one of the world's most productive crops. Widely grown for food, animal feed, biofuel and industrial starch.

tips.body: Plant after last frost when soil temperature reaches 10°C. Sow 3–4 cm deep, 20–25 cm apart.
Deep, well-drained loam or silt-loam soils with high organic matter are ideal.
Critical irrigation periods: tassel emergence and silking. Drought at these stages cuts yield sharply.
Harvest grain corn at 25% grain moisture; dry to below 14% for storage.
Fall armyworm and corn rootworm are major pests. Scout fields weekly during early vegetative stages.
Maize is a heavy nitrogen feeder. Apply split doses at planting and at the V6 growth stage.

name: Potatoes
description: A cool-season root vegetable and one of the most important food crops globally. Rich in carbohydrates, potassium and vitamin C.

tips.body: Plant certified seed tubers 10–15 cm deep, 30 cm apart in rows 75 cm wide. Chit tubers before planting.
Sandy-loam soils with good drainage and slightly acidic pH prevent scab disease.
Consistent moisture is critical from tuber initiation through bulking. Avoid wet-dry cycles that cause hollow heart.
Harvest 2–3 weeks after vines die back. Cure at 15°C and 90% humidity for 10–14 days.
Late blight (Phytophthora) is the most destructive disease. Apply fungicide preventively in wet weather.
Avoid rotating with tomatoes, peppers or other Solanaceae crops to break pest and disease cycles.

name: Sugar cane
description: A tall perennial grass grown in tropical and subtropical regions, primarily for sugar and ethanol production.

tips.body: Plant stalk cuttings (setts) with 2–3 buds at 5–10 cm depth. Rows 120–150 cm apart.
Requires high water use. Drip irrigation improves water-use efficiency significantly.
Harvest at 12–18 months when Brix reading exceeds 18% and before heavy rains dilute sucrose.
Stem borers and ratoon stunting disease are key threats. Use disease-free seed cane.
Ratoon crops (regrowth after harvest) can be maintained for 3–5 cycles before replanting.

name: Beans, dry
description: Warm-season legume rich in protein and dietary fibre. Fixes atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility for following crops.

tips.body: Sow after last frost at 3–4 cm depth. Inoculate seeds with Rhizobium bacteria for nitrogen fixation.
Well-drained soils essential. Waterlogging for even 24 hours can cause root rot.
Harvest when 90% of pods are dry and seeds rattle inside. Thresh immediately to avoid weathering.
Bean weevil in storage is a major problem. Dry to below 14% moisture and store in sealed containers.
Excellent rotation crop — residual nitrogen from nodules benefits the following cereal crop.

name: Chickpeas
description: A drought-tolerant cool-season legume with high protein content. Important in South Asian and Mediterranean diets.

tips.body: Sow in cool season at 5 cm depth. Works well after wheat in rotation.
Generally grown rainfed. Supplemental irrigation at flowering and pod fill increases yield substantially.
Ascochyta blight is the most devastating disease. Use resistant varieties and fungicide seed treatment.
Fixes up to 140 kg N/ha — one of the most nitrogen-productive legume crops.

name: Lentils
description: A fast-maturing cool-season legume highly valued for protein. One of the earliest crops domesticated by humans.

tips.body: Sow early spring at 2–3 cm depth. Shallow planting improves emergence in cool soils.
Harvest when lower pods turn brown. Delayed harvest causes shattering losses.
Very water-efficient — a key crop in semi-arid regions where water is scarce.

name: Soybeans
description: A warm-season legume and the world's most widely grown oilseed. Key source of protein for animal feed and vegetable oil.

tips.body: Plant when soil temperature exceeds 15°C. Inoculate with Bradyrhizobium japonicum.
Most sensitive to drought during pod-fill stage. One stress event here can cut yield by 40%.
Soybean cyst nematode is the most yield-limiting pest. Use resistant varieties and 3-year rotation.
Day-length sensitive — choose varieties matched to your latitude for optimal flowering timing.

name: Groundnuts, excluding shelled
description: Also known as peanuts. A warm-season legume that develops its pods underground. Rich in protein and oils.

tips.body: Plant hulled seeds 4–5 cm deep. Sandy soils allow easy pegging and pod development.
Loose, sandy-loam soils are critical — heavy clay soils impede pod development and harvesting.
Aflatoxin contamination occurs in drought stress. Ensure adequate moisture especially at pegging.
Harvest when foliage yellows and inner pod walls show dark veining. Dry to below 10% moisture.

name: Olives
description: An ancient slow-maturing evergreen tree native to the Mediterranean. Grown primarily for olive oil and table olives.

tips.body: Plant grafted trees in spring, spaced 6–8 m apart. Trees take 3–5 years to bear fruit commercially.
Very drought-tolerant once established. Deficit irrigation during fruit development improves oil quality.
Harvest for oil when olives turn from green to purple (veraison). Table olives harvested green or black.
Olive fly (Bactrocera oleae) is the main pest. Monitor with pheromone traps from July.
Olives require cold winter temperatures (chilling) for flower bud induction. Not suited to tropical climates.

name: Sunflower seed
description: A tall annual plant grown for its oil-rich seeds. One of the most efficient oilseed crops in semi-arid regions.

tips.body: Sow at 3–4 cm depth when soil temperature reaches 8–10°C. Space 60–75 cm between rows.
Most sensitive to drought at flowering and seed-fill. Deep taproot improves drought performance.
Sclerotinia stem rot is the primary disease. Avoid fields with history of infection. 4-year rotation minimum.
Harvest when back of head turns yellow-brown and seeds test at 15% moisture or below.
Sunflower is allelopathic — its residues inhibit germination of some following crops. Plan rotation carefully.

name: Rapeseed
description: Also called canola. A cool-season oilseed crop producing a light edible oil low in saturated fats.

tips.body: Sow in late summer/early autumn for winter canola, or early spring for spring types. Very small seed — shallow sowing at 1–2 cm.
Sclerotinia, blackleg and flea beetle are major threats. Use fungicide seed treatment and monitor early.
Straight-cut when pods are mature but before shattering begins. Harvest at 9–10% seed moisture.
3-year minimum rotation to manage clubroot and blackleg disease build-up in soil.

name: Coffee, green
description: A tropical shrub grown for its seeds (beans) which are roasted to make coffee. Requires specific altitude, humidity and shade conditions.

tips.body: Plant seedlings from nursery at 18–24 months old. Space 2.5–3 m between plants.
Requires well-drained, deep volcanic loam rich in organic matter. Avoid compacted or waterlogged soils.
Irrigation is critical in dry seasons to maintain flowering and fruit set consistency.
Selectively pick only ripe red cherries for premium quality. Mechanical stripping reduces quality but cuts costs.
Shade trees reduce heat stress, improve bean quality and support biodiversity. Essential above 1200 m altitude.

name: Cocoa beans
description: A tropical evergreen tree producing pods containing cocoa seeds used to make chocolate. Requires humid, equatorial conditions.

tips.body: Plant in shade of taller trees. Young trees cannot tolerate direct sun or strong winds.
Requires consistent rainfall year-round. Any dry period exceeding 3 months reduces yield sharply.
Black pod disease (Phytophthora) is the most damaging disease globally. Remove infected pods promptly.
Harvest pods when they turn yellow or orange. Cut with machete — never pull pods off trees.
Fermentation and drying of beans are critical post-harvest steps that develop chocolate flavour.

name: Tea
description: An evergreen shrub native to Asia, grown for its leaves used to make the world's most consumed beverage after water.

tips.body: Requires strongly acidic, well-drained soils rich in organic matter. Cannot tolerate alkaline or waterlogged conditions.
Plant rooted cuttings from clonal propagation. New planting takes 3–4 years to reach commercial production.
Pluck the terminal bud and top two leaves (two-and-a-bud). Flush every 7–14 days in season.
High altitude (800–2000 m), cooler temperatures and distinct dry season produce the finest quality teas.

name: Tomatoes
description: A warm-season fruiting vegetable and one of the world's most cultivated and consumed horticultural crops.

tips.body: Transplant 6–8 week old seedlings after last frost. Plant deep — up to 2/3 of stem can be buried.
Well-drained loam with ample compost. Consistent soil moisture prevents blossom-end rot.
Drip irrigation to leaves dry reduces fungal disease. Consistent watering prevents cracking.
Pick when fully coloured and slightly soft. Vine-ripened tomatoes have superior flavour.
Late blight, early blight and tomato mosaic virus are key threats. Stake plants and ensure good airflow.
Rotate tomatoes out of Solanaceae family (peppers, potatoes, eggplant) for minimum 3 years.

name: Cauliflowers and broccoli
description: Cool-season brassica vegetables grown for their dense edible flower heads. Very nutritious and high in vitamins C and K.

tips.body: Transplant seedlings 4–6 weeks before last spring frost or 6–8 weeks before first autumn frost.
Needs consistent soil moisture and fertility. Boron deficiency causes hollow stem in broccoli.
Harvest cauliflower before curds separate; harvest broccoli before yellow flowers open.
Caterpillars (diamondback moth, cabbage worm) are primary pests. Use Bt spray or row covers.

name: Cabbages
description: A leafy cool-season vegetable in the brassica family. One of the oldest cultivated vegetables with strong cold tolerance.

tips.body: Start transplants 6–8 weeks indoors. Can tolerate light frost but protect from hard freeze.
Needs fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Add lime if pH is below 6.0 to reduce clubroot risk.
Clubroot is the most damaging disease. Raise soil pH to 7.2 to suppress it in affected fields.
Harvest when heads are firm and solid. Delayed harvest causes splitting, especially after rain.

name: Garlic
description: A bulb crop in the allium family, widely used as a culinary flavouring and for its medicinal properties.

tips.body: Plant individual cloves in autumn at 5 cm depth, 15 cm apart. Pointy end up.
Harvest when half the leaves have turned brown and fallen. Cure in a dry airy location for 3–4 weeks.
Garlic needs a cold vernalisation period (below 10°C for 4–8 weeks) to form separate cloves.

name: Onions, dry
description: A bulb vegetable essential in cuisines worldwide. Long-day varieties suit northern latitudes; short-day types suit the tropics.

tips.body: Direct-seed or transplant. Shallow planting at 1–2 cm ensures good emergence.
Harvest when tops have fallen over. Cure in the field or in a well-ventilated shed before storage.
Reduce irrigation 2 weeks before harvest to improve skin quality and storage life.

name: Cucumbers and gherkins
description: A warm-season cucurbit grown for its tender green fruit. Requires warm temperatures and consistent moisture.

tips.body: Direct-seed after last frost at 2–3 cm depth. Trellis for better airflow and straighter fruit.
Harvest slicing cucumbers at 15–20 cm; gherkins at 5–8 cm. Pick frequently to stimulate production.
Powdery mildew and cucumber beetles are key threats. Rotate away from cucurbits for 2 years.

name: Eggplants (aubergines)
description: A warm-season vegetable in the Solanaceae family, grown for its large, glossy purple fruit. Requires a long, warm growing season.

tips.body: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost. Transplant when night temperatures stay above 15°C.
Consistent moisture is essential. Water stress during flowering causes blossom drop and bitter fruit.
Harvest when skin is glossy and firm. Over-ripe fruit becomes seedy, bitter and loses quality rapidly.
Flea beetles and Colorado potato beetle are major pests. Use row covers early in the season.
Rotate out of Solanaceae family (tomatoes, peppers, potatoes) for 3 years minimum.

name: Chillies and peppers, green
description: Warm-season fruiting vegetables ranging from sweet bell peppers to very hot chillies. Rich in vitamin C and capsaicin.

tips.body: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost. Germination is slow; soil temperature should exceed 22°C.
Consistent moisture needed but avoid waterlogging. Drip irrigation reduces disease incidence.
Harvest green peppers when full-sized; allow to ripen red/yellow for sweeter, more nutritious fruit.
Phytophthora root rot is a major threat. Avoid replanting in affected areas for several years.

name: Watermelons
description: A large cucurbit fruit grown in warm climates for its sweet, watery flesh. One of the most heat-tolerant vegetable crops.

tips.body: Direct-seed or transplant after last frost into sandy soils. Space vines 1.5–2 m apart.
Harvest when the ground spot turns from white to cream-yellow and the nearest tendril dries out.
Reduce irrigation when fruit begins ripening to concentrate sugars and improve flavour.

name: Zucchini (courgettes)
description: A highly productive warm-season summer squash harvested young. One of the fastest vegetables from seed to harvest.

tips.body: Sow directly after last frost at 2–3 cm. Germination is fast. Plant 3–4 seeds and thin to 1–2 plants.
Harvest at 15–20 cm length every 2–3 days. Left to grow large, fruit becomes seedy and less flavourful.
Powdery mildew and vine borers are the main threats. Choose mildew-resistant varieties.
Extremely productive — one plant can produce 8–10 kg over the season. Pollination requires bee activity.

name: Peas, green
description: A cool-season legume grown for fresh or frozen green peas. One of the first crops sown in spring.

tips.body: Direct-sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring. Peas tolerate light frost but not heat.
Harvest when pods are plump but peas still bright green. Check daily — they over-mature quickly.
Stop producing quickly in heat. Succession-plant every 2 weeks for continuous harvest.

name: Bananas
description: A tropical perennial monocot grown for its nutritious fruit. The world's most popular fruit and a staple food in tropical regions.

tips.body: Plant suckers or tissue-cultured plants in pits 60×60×60 cm with compost. Space 2×2.5 m.
Bananas are the highest water-consuming crop. Drip irrigation is highly recommended.
Fusarium wilt (Panama disease) TR4 is a global threat. Use certified disease-free planting material.
Harvest green when fruit is full and angular edges become rounded. Ripen off the plant.
After harvest, cut the main stem down and allow ratoon suckers to produce the next crop.

name: Oranges
description: A subtropical citrus fruit tree producing one of the world's most consumed juices. Requires warm winters and distinct seasons.

tips.body: Plant grafted trees on certified rootstock. Space 5–6 m apart. Avoid frost pockets.
Deficit irrigation during fruit development concentrates sugars and improves juice quality.
Citrus greening (HLB) is an incurable bacterial disease spread by psyllid insects. Monitor and control vectors.
Pick when Brix/acid ratio reaches target — oranges do not continue to ripen off the tree.

name: Avocados
description: A subtropical tree producing a nutrient-dense buttery fruit rich in healthy fats. Requires well-drained soils and mild, frost-free conditions.

tips.body: Avocados are extremely sensitive to waterlogging. Even brief flooding can kill roots within hours. Perfect drainage is essential.
Plant grafted trees on rootstocks tolerant to Phytophthora root rot. Raise planting beds if drainage is imperfect.
Requires consistent irrigation but any waterlogging is fatal. Microjet or drip systems on raised beds.
Phytophthora root rot is the primary threat. Maintain soil health with mulching and avoid any puddling.
Avocados ripen only after picking. Harvest when oil content exceeds 8–10% (variety dependent).

name: Grapes
description: A woody perennial vine grown for table fruit, wine, juice and raisins. Wine grapes require specific climate conditions for optimal quality.

tips.body: Plant dormant cuttings or grafted vines on disease-resistant rootstocks in winter. Train to trellis system.
Annual winter pruning is essential to control crop load and canopy structure for quality fruit.
Deficit irrigation from veraison (colour change) onwards stresses vines to concentrate sugars and flavour compounds.
Botrytis bunch rot is critical in wet climates. Keep canopy open and avoid nitrogen excess.
Harvest date is critical. Sample Brix, pH and taste regularly in the final 3 weeks before picking.
Wine grape quality is strongly influenced by soil type. Stressed vines on lean soils often produce better wine than fertile ones.

name: Apples
description: A temperate deciduous tree producing one of the world's most widely grown fruits. Requires winter chilling for dormancy and fruit set.

tips.body: Plant on dwarfing or semi-dwarfing rootstocks for intensive systems. Requires cross-pollination — plant two compatible varieties.
Consistent moisture during fruit cell division (first 6 weeks after petal fall) determines final fruit size.
Codling moth is the primary insect pest worldwide. Use pheromone traps and targeted treatments.
Test harvest readiness with starch-iodine index, firmness and Brix. Each variety has a specific harvest window.
Requires 800–1200 hours below 7°C (chilling hours) for proper dormancy break and flower bud development.

name: Strawberries
description: A low-growing perennial fruit plant producing sweet red berries. One of the most popular soft fruits, grown worldwide.

tips.body: Plant certified disease-free runners in raised beds in late summer for spring fruit, or autumn-planted for summer.
Sandy or sandy-loam soils with good drainage. Fumigation may be needed in replant sites.
Drip irrigation maintains dry foliage and reduces Botrytis risk. Consistent moisture critical during fruiting.
Botrytis grey mould in wet seasons; spider mites in hot dry conditions. Monitor weekly.
Pick when fully red all around the fruit. Strawberries do not ripen further after picking.
Renew plantings every 3–4 years. Runners left in beds reduce yields and harbour disease.

name: Almonds, in shell
description: A deciduous tree in the rose family producing one of the world's most nutritious and consumed nuts. Requires a Mediterranean climate.

tips.body: Plant grafted trees on rootstocks suited to your soil. Almonds bloom very early — avoid frost-prone sites.
Very drought-tolerant once established. However, nut size and yield respond strongly to irrigation during hull split.
Navel orangeworm is the major nut pest. Timely harvest and orchard sanitation are the primary controls.
Harvest when 95–100% of hulls have split. Shake trees and windrow nuts for mechanical pickup.
Almonds bloom 3–4 weeks before other tree crops — a single frost event during bloom can eliminate the entire crop.

name: Walnuts, in shell
description: A large deciduous tree producing nutritious nuts rich in omega-3 fatty acids. Trees are long-lived and increase in productivity for decades.

tips.body: Plant grafted English walnut on Persian or Northern California Black walnut rootstock. Deep, fertile soil is essential.
Walnuts are allelopathic — juglone from roots suppresses many other plants. Keep susceptible crops well away.
Codling moth and walnut blight (bacterial) are the primary threats. Begin spray programme at bud-break.
Harvest when hulls split and nuts can be shaken free. Dry promptly to 8% moisture to prevent mould.
